I don't think this class is safe. It looks like you are using a single event loop from multiple threads here. I'm 99% sure you shouldn't do that. (I've had exceptions thrown as a result).

Instead the event loop should be setup after thread creation like this:

```python
class AIOThread(threading.Thread):
    """
    TODO: temporary convenience class. Create an event loop and event object for
    controlling the Accessory.run method.
    """

    def __init__(self, run_method):
        """
        """
        self.run_method = run_method
        super().__init__()

    def run(self):
        self.loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
        asyncio.set_event_loop(self.loop)

        self.stop_event = asyncio.Event()
        self.task = self.loop.create_task(self.run_method(self.stop_event, self.loop))

        self.loop.run_until_complete(self.task)

    async def safe_shutdown(self):
        try:
            self.stop_event.set()
            await asyncio.sleep(5)
            if not self.task.done():
                self.task.cancel()
                await asyncio.sleep(5)
            self.loop.stop()
        finally:
            self.loop.close()
            logger.info("Sucessfully stopped accessory event loop.")

    def stop(self):
        self.loop.call_soon_threadsafe(self.safe_shutdown)
```

Optimizers are all registered at once in the original CPP-Package code and in the Python code using decorators. In the original code [#5251](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/21b2da07f6ff10669b7649860b913e17e6184708/cpp-package/include/mxnet-cpp/optimizer.hpp), they were all registered at once in the file, so when the program was initialized, all optimizers would already be registered (which is the case in Python), not in the first call to the function Find, but that caused multiple definition errors, so in #5545, the were all registered at once in the first call to Find, which caused the inability to have more than one optimizer bc they would be re-registered with each call, so this change makes sure they are registered once. Why check every optimizer separately when they should all be registered at the same time and only once?

My position is: right now we use a `_sampling_priority_v1` metric instead of the `sampling.priority` tag that would ensure OT compatibility, so my guess is all this is beta, therefore fine to keep private, we can make it explicitly public later. There are subtles implications of using an attribute it, one is: it's fine to set the service/error state later than span creation as long as no distributed child span has been created, this implies control on when `_sampling_priority` is accessed. Similarly, setting `_sampling_priority_v1` after a child, distributed trace has been triggered, will lead to clear plain bugs as the information has already be sent. All in all, this is really a use-at-your-risk member, we might some day harden it, but my expectation this should come along with OT support, typically.

Yeah, I get the thinking here, but I still think they should be the same. My reasoning goes:

If the test is flaky with the 5s timeout at 500ms, that means that sometimes it takes more than 500ms for us to wake up the thread.

Which means that waiting 500ms isn't waiting long enough to verify that we _won't_ wake up the thread, because we know that sometimes it takes longer than that.

Which means that our check which is trying to say "we never wake up the thread while the semaphore should be unavailable" needs to use a timeout bigger than 500ms to check that we never wake up the thread, because we _expect_ it to sometimes take more than 500ms (which is why the test is flaky).

So we need the values to be the same, because the amount we need to wait to convincingly guarantee that the thread _will_ be woken up is the same amount of time we need to wait to convincingly guarantee that the thread _won't_ be woken up.

Here, the issue was that I was getting the model, by the undefined id, and hence still getting a model that was cached - so by directly calling `createModel` I ensure that we get a new model, rather than one that might (later, due to a resolved promise) have an id set later.

Is it up to the app where to store, for example, claims?

I think there are 3 main options possible:
1) Only secrets (private, master secret) are stored in the wallet on libindy level. All other entities are assumed to be stored on app level.
2) Everything is stored in the wallet on libindy level. Here we assume that each libindy call works with **one** wallet only, so that by providing a proper wallet_handle, the app can decide what kind of wallet/implementation needs to be used. Here the wallet may mean something more general (like just a storage with optional security properties).

Correct; this is what I meant by "consistent mutable-default-value initialization." Default arguments are values and not expressions (cf. JavaScript), so they are evaluated at function definition time and not function invocation time. Writing `my_list=[]` is nearly always wrong, and is discouraged by Google style

> Default arguments are evaluated once at module load time. This may cause problems if the argument is a mutable object such as a list or a dictionary. If the function modifies the object (e.g., by appending an item to a list), the default value is modified.

; I recently also learned that you can write
```py
>>> def my_function(foo=[]):
...     return foo
... 
>>> my_function.func_defaults[0]
[]
>>> my_function.func_defaults = (['banana'], )
>>> my_function()
['banana']
```
which makes them even more dangerous.

The mediated transfer tasks are really coded to move the protocol forward and have no notion of undoing events, there is no logic in-place to undo the events back to the fork point. I also think it's not a good idea to add code to handle reorgs in the mediated transfer tasks, as this would mix up concerns and definitely make the protocol code even harder to grasp.

The WAL OTOH is the perfect structure to unwind events, since it keeps all the history, we only need to get the newest snapshot *before* the reorg happened, and replay all the events up to the tip of the reorg. I would like to make a `StateManager` implementation that is aware of reorgs and can (re)create tasks using the above strategy.
